refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly defines the measurement basis (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period scope, and acknowledges that availability is a system-level emergent property dependent on vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, safety service responsiveness, and maintenance effectiveness. High safety criticality requires that safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety hold, fault) across all modules; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime vs. total time; traceability to root causes of downtime events; comparison against customer-defined baseline and acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical reliability such that unplanned downtime due to component failure does not exceed 3% of operational time.

FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network link availability and command/response latency within acceptable bounds such that tele-operator-induced downtime does not exceed 1% of operational time.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within required response time, triggering safe shutdown without introducing unnecessary operational delays (safety-driven holds ≤2% of operational time).
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide predictive maintenance scheduling and health monitoring to minimize unplanned maintenance events and reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) to ≤4 hours.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystem architectures (dual Vehicle units, backup Teleoperation links, distributed Safety Services) with automatic failover to mask single-point failures and maintain availability target.

DP-B: Implement comprehensive health monitoring, predictive diagnostics, and condition-based maintenance scheduling across all subsystems to reduce unplanned downtime and optimize maintenance windows.

DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management system that continuously monitors subsystem health, predicts failures, schedules preventive maintenance, and coordinates safe shutdown protocols to meet the 0.97 availability target.
DP-D: Implement modular, hot-swappable component design with rapid replacement procedures and spare parts inventory management to minimize MTTR and unplanned downtime.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hierarchical availability management system that continuously monitors subsystem health (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), predicts failures using telemetry data, schedules preventive maintenance during planned windows, and coordinates safe shutdown protocols to meet the 0.97 availability target.

potential_sources: DP-C depends on real-time telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems (Telemetry interface).
DP-C must coordinate with Safety Services to ensure safety-driven shutdowns are logged as planned downtime, not failures (SafetyAlert interface).
DP-C must interface with maintenance scheduling and spare parts management (external to current scope but assumed available).
DP-C does not directly couple to Vehicle or Teleoperation operational logic; it observes and coordinates, reducing architectural coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is a monitoring and coordination pattern that decouples availability management from subsystem implementation details. It requires well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) but does not mandate redundancy or specific component choices, allowing flexibility in subsystem design.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Telemetry data from all subsystems is available with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable predictive diagnostics.
Assumption: Maintenance scheduling can be coordinated with operational demands (customer-defined evaluation period allows planned maintenance windows).
Risk: If telemetry is incomplete or delayed, predictive diagnostics may fail, reducing effectiveness of DP-C.
Risk: If subsystems lack health monitoring instrumentation, DP-C cannot function; subsystem design must include health sensors and reporting.

Risk: Availability target of 0.97 may be unachievable if subsystem MTBF (mean time between failures) is too low; DP-C assumes subsystems are inherently reliable enough that monitoring and maintenance can bridge the gap.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) telemetry data requirements (sampling rate, latency, accuracy) for each subsystem; (2) predictive diagnostic algorithms and their failure prediction accuracy targets; (3) maintenance scheduling constraints and optimization criteria; (4) safe shutdown coordination protocol with Safety Services. This will enable clearer IM assignment and verification.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: The Safety Services module is assigned primary ownership of the availability management system (DP-C). It shall implement health monitoring, predictive diagnostics, maintenance scheduling coordination, and safe shutdown protocols. Safety Services is the logical owner because: (1) it already owns safety-critical decision-making and shutdown authority; (2) availability is a safety property (unplanned downtime can create hazardous conditions); (3) it has natural interfaces to all other subsystems via SafetyAlert and Telemetry; (4) it can coordinate maintenance windows without disrupting operational safety.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Continuous health and status data from Vehicle (mechanical/electrical state, component temperatures, vibration, fuel/battery levels) and Teleoperation (network latency, link quality, command success rate). Used by Safety Services to detect anomalies and predict failures.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services sends SafetyAlert to Vehicle and Teleoperation to trigger safe shutdown when hazardous conditions are detected or predicted. Vehicle and Teleoperation send SafetyAlert back to Safety Services to confirm shutdown state and report safety-critical events.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives operational tasking context from NGHE Solution to understand planned maintenance windows and operational schedules, enabling coordination of maintenance with mission planning.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services monitors Network Link quality and availability (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) to assess Teleoperation subsystem health and predict communication failures.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator and could own availability management as a cross-cutting concern. However, assigning DP-C to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple subsystems and create tight coupling to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services internals. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle is responsible for mechanical/electrical operation, not system-level availability coordination. Assigning DP-C to Vehicle would create high diffusion (Vehicle would need to monitor Teleoperation and Safety Services health) and violate separation of concerns. Not suitable.

Teleoperation is responsible for remote command and control, not system-level availability. Assigning DP-C to Teleoperation would create diffusion and coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services. Not suitable.

Safety Services is the natural owner of availability management because: (1) it already monitors system health for safety purposes; (2) it has authority to trigger safe shutdowns; (3) it has natural interfaces to all subsystems (Telemetry, SafetyAlert); (4) availability is a safety property; (5) it can coordinate maintenance without diffusing responsibility. High containment, low diffusion. Recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services can fully contain the availability management system (DP-C) because it already owns safety-critical monitoring and decision-making. By extending its health monitoring scope to include predictive diagnostics and maintenance scheduling coordination, Safety Services can implement DP-C with minimal architectural diffusion. The required interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are already defined or can be naturally extended.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become overloaded with dual responsibility (safety + availability). Mitigation: Clearly separate safety-critical logic (hazard detection, shutdown) from availability optimization (predictive maintenance, scheduling). Use distinct code modules within Safety Services if needed.

Risk: Predictive diagnostics algorithms may require domain expertise not present in Safety Services team. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contracts for telemetry data and diagnostic outputs; allow external diagnostic engines to feed into Safety Services decision logic.

Risk: Maintenance scheduling coordination may require integration with external maintenance management systems (not in scope). Mitigation: Define Tasking interface to include maintenance scheduling requests and confirmations; assume external system provides scheduling constraints.

Risk: If Safety Services is already resource-constrained, adding availability management may impact safety-critical response time. Mitigation: Implement availability management as lower-priority background tasks; ensure safety-critical paths are not blocked by availability monitoring.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Safety Services IM scope to explicitly separate: (1) Safety-Critical Module: hazard detection, shutdown authority, emergency response (existing); (2) Availability Management Module: health monitoring, predictive diagnostics, maintenance coordination (new). Define clear internal interfaces between these modules to prevent coupling. Specify telemetry data requirements and diagnostic algorithm contracts to enable independent development and testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hierarchical availability management system) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP specifies a monitoring and coordination approach that reduces unplanned downtime through predictive diagnostics and maintenance scheduling, and coordinates safe shutdowns to ensure safety-driven holds are counted as planned downtime. The DP is logically sound and feasible. No decoupling issues detected at FR-DP level.

diffusion_risks: Safety Services must integrate telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation without creating tight coupling. Mitigation: Use well-defined Telemetry interface with schema versioning to allow subsystem evolution.

Safety Services must coordinate maintenance scheduling with external systems (assumed out-of-scope). Mitigation: Define Tasking interface contract clearly; assume external system provides scheduling constraints and confirmations.

Predictive diagnostics algorithms may require updates as subsystems evolve. Mitigation: Implement diagnostic logic as pluggable modules within Safety Services; use interface contracts to decouple algorithm implementation from data sources.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary for DP-C. It has natural authority (safety-critical decision-making), required interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link), and minimal diffusion risk. The assignment is architecturally sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible through continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Safety Services can log all state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety hold, fault) and compute availability ratio. Traceability to root causes of downtime events is achievable via telemetry correlation and incident reports.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004 is allocated to Safety Services via DP-C. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution respectively. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify telemetry data requirements (sampling rate, latency, accuracy) for each subsystem. This will enable clearer verification and subsystem design constraints.

Refine DP-C to specify predictive diagnostic algorithms and their failure prediction accuracy targets (e.g., 'detect 80% of failures ≥24 hours before occurrence'). This will enable validation of DP-C effectiveness.
Refine Safety Services IM scope to explicitly separate safety-critical logic from availability management logic. Define internal module boundaries and interfaces to prevent coupling.
Define Tasking interface contract to include maintenance scheduling requests, constraints, and confirmations. Clarify assumptions about external maintenance management system.

Establish acceptance criteria for availability measurement: define evaluation period duration, acceptable downtime categories (planned maintenance, safety holds, unplanned failures), and statistical confidence level for 0.97 target.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to enable detailed design and verification. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to Safety Services is architecturally justified. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) require further decomposition to specify design parameters for Vehicle reliability, Teleoperation robustness, Safety Services responsiveness, and maintenance optimization. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will enable clearer allocation of design responsibility and verification criteria.
decision.justification_detail: The current level establishes the availability management strategy (DP-C) and assigns it to Safety Services. The next level must decompose each sub-FR into specific design parameters and implementation modules. For example, FR-NGHE-004.1 (Vehicle reliability) must be decomposed into DPs for component selection, redundancy, thermal management, and predictive maintenance. This will enable Vehicle subsystem design to be driven by availability requirements rather than generic reliability targets.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical reliability such that unplanned downtime due to component failure does not exceed 3% of operational time.

FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network link availability and command/response latency within acceptable bounds such that tele-operator-induced downtime does not exceed 1% of operational time.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within required response time, triggering safe shutdown without introducing unnecessary operational delays (safety-driven holds ≤2% of operational time).
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide predictive maintenance scheduling and health monitoring to minimize unplanned maintenance events and reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) to ≤4 hours.

assumptions: Telemetry data from all subsystems is available with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable predictive diagnostics.
Maintenance scheduling can be coordinated with operational demands; customer-defined evaluation period allows planned maintenance windows.
Subsystems are inherently reliable enough that monitoring and maintenance can bridge the gap to 0.97 availability target.
Safety Services has sufficient computational and communication resources to implement availability management without impacting safety-critical response time.
External maintenance management system exists and can be integrated via Tasking interface.
Safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures, in availability calculation.
open_risks: If telemetry is incomplete or delayed, predictive diagnostics may fail, reducing effectiveness of DP-C.
If subsystems lack health monitoring instrumentation, DP-C cannot function; subsystem design must include health sensors and reporting.
If subsystem MTBF is too low, availability target of 0.97 may be unachievable.
Safety Services may become overloaded with dual responsibility (safety + availability), impacting safety-critical response time.
Predictive diagnostics algorithms may require domain expertise not present in Safety Services team.
Maintenance scheduling coordination may require integration with external systems not in scope.
evidence_needed: Telemetry data schema and interface specification (sampling rate, latency, accuracy requirements).
Predictive diagnostic algorithm specifications and failure prediction accuracy targets.
Subsystem MTBF and MTTR estimates to validate feasibility of 0.97 availability target.
Safety Services resource allocation and response time analysis to ensure availability management does not impact safety-critical paths.
Maintenance management system integration plan and Tasking interface contract.
Availability measurement methodology and acceptance criteria (evaluation period, downtime categories, statistical confidence).
